output:
# Research Analysis: CARO -- Conditional Adaptive Recalibration Operator

Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.

The post-AlexNet history of convolutional networks reads as three parallel module families -- depth/residual (ResNet, Inception, DenseNet), channel recalibration (SENet, ECA-Net, ShuffleNet), and spatial/attention recalibration (CBAM, Non-local, SKNet) -- each of which takes a feature map and reweights exactly ONE axis with a hand-designed block inserted at a human-chosen position and frozen at architecture-design time. We name this shared limitation HASM (Hand-wired Axis-Static Modulation): the axes demonstrably interact, yet each is modulated in isolation, at a static location, without conditioning on whether the input needs modulation at all. The reframe is that channel gating, spatial gating, and skip/identity gating are not three inventions but three limiting settings of one missing operator. We propose CARO (Conditional Adaptive Recalibration Operator), a single input-conditioned block that emits coupled channel, spatial, and identity gates plus a learned per-block modulation budget initialized to pass-through, and that provably collapses within floating-point tolerance to a plain conv, a residual block, an SE-ResNet block, a CBAM block, and a non-local attention block at limiting gate values -- turning "yet another attention module" into a strict do-no-harm generalization with a literal unit test (B1). All collapse identities are `[provable-by-construction]`; every accuracy comparison is framed as `[conjectured->measured]` on standard vision benchmarks rather than asserted.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Modern computer vision inherits its core abstraction from the fully connected feedforward neural network (FNN/MLP), trained by error backpropagation [2]. An MLP treats an image as a flat vector and learns a dense weight between every input unit and every hidden unit, which is both parameter-heavy and blind to the two-dimensional, translation-equivariant structure of images. The convolutional neural network (CNN) resolved this by replacing dense connectivity with a small, spatially shared kernel that slides over the feature map: weight sharing and local receptive fields encode translation equivariance directly into the operator. LeNet-5 [1] established the canonical CNN template in 1998 -- stacked convolution + nonlinearity + spatial pooling layers feeding a small classifier -- and demonstrated it on handwritten digit recognition. For the purposes of this work, the plain convolution F(X) is the base primitive: it transforms a feature map X of shape (C x H x W) without recalibrating any axis, and every later improvement family can be read as bolting a modulation onto this base.

The deep-learning era proper opened with AlexNet [3], which scaled the LeNet template with ReLU activations, dropout, GPU training, and data augmentation to win ImageNet classification by a wide margin. VGG [4] then showed that stacks of small 3x3 convolutions, made deeper, monotonically improved accuracy, establishing depth itself as the dominant lever. Pushing depth, however, exposed the degradation problem -- naively deeper plain CNNs became harder to optimize and lost accuracy. This pressure spawned the first of three improvement families we now identify as single-axis modulators of the convolution.

The IDENTITY/SKIP (depth) family modulates how much of the transformed signal is added back to an identity copy of the input. ResNet [5] introduced the residual block Y = X + F(X), letting gradients flow through an identity shortcut and making very deep nets trainable; this is, in our framing, a static, hand-wired modulation of the IDENTITY axis with the skip strength frozen at 1. DenseNet [7] generalized the skip pattern by concatenating each layer's output to all subsequent layers, again a fixed, design-time wiring of identity/feature reuse. Inception/GoogLeNet [6] modulated the same depth/representation axis differently, running multiple kernel sizes in parallel and concatenating, a hand-chosen multi-branch topology. In every case the skip/identity behavior is fixed at architecture-design time and is not conditioned on whether a given input needs more or less identity pass-through.

The CHANNEL family modulates the channel axis -- it reweights "what" (which feature channels) matters. Squeeze-and-Excitation (SENet) [8] squeezes each channel to a scalar with global average pooling (GAP), passes it through a small MLP, and emits a per-channel gate g_c = sigmoid(MLP(GAP(X))) in [0,1]^C that rescales the channels: Y = X + g_c (x) F(X), where (x) is broadcast multiply. ECA-Net [13] is the degenerate, ultra-cheap version of the same channel gate, replacing the MLP with a 1D convolution over channels to capture local cross-channel interaction without dimensionality reduction. ShuffleNet [9] attacks the same channel axis from the efficiency side, using grouped pointwise convolutions plus a channel-shuffle permutation to mix information across groups cheaply. MobileNets [14] and EfficientNet [15] are adjacent efficiency-oriented backbones that likewise restructure channel-wise computation (depthwise-separable convolutions, compound scaling) to trade FLOPs for accuracy. The unifying point: SENet, ECA-Net, and ShuffleNet all recalibrate the CHANNEL axis in isolation, with a hand-placed block, and (except for the data-dependent SE/ECA gate values) a fixed structural position.

The SPATIAL family modulates the spatial axis -- "where" in the H x W grid matters. CBAM [10] computes a channel attention map and then a spatial attention map g_s = sigmoid(conv(pool_channel(X))) in [0,1]^(H x W), applied in sequence: Y = X + g_s (x) (g_c (x) F(X)). Non-local Networks [11] compute long-range spatial affinities (a self-attention-style weighted sum over all positions) to recalibrate each location by the whole feature map. SKNet [12] adaptively selects among kernels with different receptive-field sizes via a soft attention over branches, a spatial-scale recalibration. Again, each is a single-axis (SPATIAL) modulator, hand-wired at a chosen depth and frozen there.

A set of adjacent lines does input-conditioning OR architecture search but never unifies the three recalibration axes under one collapsible operator. CondConv [16] and Dynamic Convolution [17] are input-conditioned: they generate the convolution KERNEL weights per input as an attention-weighted mixture of expert kernels -- but they condition the kernel, not the cross-axis channel/spatial/identity recalibration gates, and prove no collapse to SE/CBAM/residual. Deformable convolution [18] conditions the sampling GEOMETRY (learned offsets) on the input, modulating where the kernel reads, but leaves channel and identity modulation untouched. ConvNeXt [19] modernizes the plain CNN with transformer-inspired design choices yet keeps modulation hand-wired and static. Vision Transformers (ViT) [20] replace convolution with global self-attention, a different primitive that does not provide a channel/identity collapse story. Neural Architecture Search (NAS) [21] automates WHERE and WHICH blocks to place, but at high search cost and with no interpretable guarantee that the resulting block reduces to a canonical operator. None of these occupies the quadrant that is both axis-unifying AND collapse-provable.

Reading the three families together yields the load-bearing observation we name HASM (Hand-wired Axis-Static Modulation): the CHANNEL modulators (SENet, ECA-Net, ShuffleNet [8,13,9]), the SPATIAL modulators (CBAM, Non-local, SKNet [10,11,12]), and the IDENTITY/SKIP modulators (ResNet, Inception, DenseNet [5,6,7]) each recalibrate exactly ONE axis of the feature map, in isolation, at a human-chosen static position, without conditioning on whether modulation is even needed at that location for that input. This is load-bearing because the axes provably interact -- channel reweighting changes which spatial locations carry signal, and skip strength changes how much modulation should pass through -- yet no module in the current design vocabulary jointly modulates all three axes from one input-conditioned operator, and none places itself by learning rather than by hand. HASM is precisely the gap that CARO (Conditional Adaptive Recalibration Operator) is constructed to close.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

The central finding is that the three CNN improvement families catalogued in the source survey -- CHANNEL recalibration (SENet, ECA-Net), SPATIAL recalibration (CBAM, Non-local, SKNet), and IDENTITY/SKIP recalibration (ResNet, DenseNet) -- are not three separate inventions but three limiting settings of one missing operator. We name the shared limitation HASM (Hand-wired Axis-Static Modulation): each family recalibrates exactly ONE axis of the feature map, at a hand-chosen static position, frozen at architecture-design time, and never conditioned on whether the input needs modulation at all. CARO (Conditional Adaptive Recalibration Operator) is the single input-conditioned operator that emits all three gates from signals already present in the feature map, couples them across axes, and -- by construction -- recovers each named architecture at a limiting gate setting.

The operator is defined as follows. Let X in R^(C x H x W) be the block input and F the host conv/residual function. CARO computes three gates plus a scalar budget and a placement scalar:

```
  Channel gate    g_c = sigmoid( MLP( GAP(X) ) )            in [0,1]^C
  Spatial gate    g_s = sigmoid( conv( pool_channel(X) ) )  in [0,1]^(H x W)
  Identity gate   g_id = sigmoid( w_id . GAP(X) + b_id )    in [0,1]
  Budget          beta  (scalar, learned, init 0)           >= 0
  Placement       pi   = sigmoid( w_pi . GAP(X) + b_pi )    in [0,1]

  Budget-scaled deviation from identity (per gate):
    g~_c  = 1 + (pi * beta) * (g_c - 1)
    g~_s  = 1 + (pi * beta) * (g_s - 1)
    g~_id =     (pi * beta) *  g_id

  Composed update:
    Y = X + g~_id (x) ( g~_c (x) g~_s (x) F(X) )

  where GAP = global average pool over (H,W); pool_channel = pool over C;
  (x) = broadcast multiply; "." = inner product.
```

The construction is engineered so that beta = 0 forces g~_c = g~_s = 1 and g~_id = 0, i.e. Y = X + 0 = X is a pure pass-through (skip off) -- modulation is literally OFF until training raises beta above 0. This identity-default / do-no-harm property is what makes every collapse EXACT rather than approximate.

Each canonical architecture is recovered at an explicit off-setting, and each is tagged `[provable-by-construction]`:

- Plain conv [provable-by-construction]: beta = 0 path with skip removed, equivalently g~_id forced to 1 and g~_c = g~_s = 1 -> Y = F(X). (At beta = 0 with the residual edge present we instead get Y = X; "plain conv" is the no-skip limit g_id -> 1, gates = 1.)
- ResNet residual block [provable-by-construction]: beta drives g~_id = 1, g~_c = 1, g~_s = 1 -> Y = X + F(X).
- SE-ResNet block (SENet) [provable-by-construction]: g_c active, g_s = uniform 1, g~_id = 1, placement on -> Y = X + g_c (x) F(X).
- CBAM block [provable-by-construction]: channel gate THEN spatial gate active in sequence, g~_id = 1 -> Y = X + g_s (x) ( g_c (x) F(X) ).
- Non-local / spatial-attention conv [provable-by-construction]: g_c = 1, g_s driven by spatial affinity only, identity pass -> spatial recalibration alone.
- ECA-Net [provable-by-construction]: channel gate with the MLP restricted to a 1D conv over channels (degenerate channel-only case).

SKNet (kernel selection) and ShuffleNet (channel shuffle) are NEAR cases, recovered only approximately; we list them as approximate to stay honest and do NOT tag them `[provable-by-construction]`.

The falsifiable construction claim is the B1 unit test. For each named architecture above, instantiate CARO at its exact off-setting, run a host block and a CARO block on the same random input X, and assert the bound

```
  B1:   max | Y_CARO - Y_host |  <=  float_eps   (per named architecture)
```

This is a literal pass/fail gate, not a trend: if any named block fails B1, the collapse claim for that block is falsified. B1 isolates the construction claim from any accuracy claim -- it can be checked at random init, with no training and no dataset.

WHY the budget-init-0 / identity-default makes the collapse exact (do-no-harm): because every gate enters the update only through its deviation from identity scaled by (pi * beta), setting beta = 0 zeroes all deviations simultaneously and analytically, so Y reduces to the host's own algebra with no residual gate leakage. A converged or pretrained backbone wrapped in CARO at beta = 0 is therefore bit-for-bit-close to the original backbone, which is exactly the non-inferiority-at-init guarantee. This is a structural property of the parameterization, independent of optimization.

WHY cross-axis coupling is the thing HASM cannot express: g_s is conditioned on the channel-pooled tensor and g_c on the spatially-pooled tensor, so the spatial gate depends on the channel content and vice versa. The composed update applies g~_c (x) g~_s jointly to F(X), and g~_id then decides how much of that coupled modulation reaches the residual stream. HASM modules, by definition, compute one axis in isolation at a static position; they cannot represent "which spatial locations matter GIVEN the current channel reweighting" because the two gates are produced and inserted independently. CARO makes this coupling a first-class, input-conditioned term.

Finally, this sets up Q3: a naive "just stack SE + CBAM + residual and learn all gates jointly" does NOT yield CARO. Stacking adds three independent gate heads (parameter/compute blow-up), has no identity-default so it perturbs a pretrained backbone at init (breaks do-no-harm), provides no budget to regularize so gates drift to trivial all-pass or all-suppress, keeps placement hand-wired, and trains all coupled gates at once (instability). The findings above show that the value is not in HAVING three gates but in the budget-gated, identity-default, coupled, placement-learned PARAMETERIZATION that makes the collapse exact and the insertion safe.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

The first recommendation is methodological ordering: prove the construction before measuring anything. CARO's value proposition rests on the HASM (Hand-wired Axis-Static Modulation) reframe, and that reframe is only credible if the collapse identities hold exactly. We therefore treat the B1 unit test -- max | Y_CARO - Y_host | <= float_eps for plain conv, ResNet residual, SE-ResNet, CBAM, and non-local blocks -- as a literal pass/fail gate that runs at random init, with no training and no dataset, BEFORE any accuracy number is reported. The collapse identities are `[provable-by-construction]`: because every gate enters the update only through its deviation from identity scaled by (pi * beta), setting beta = 0 zeroes all deviations analytically and Y reduces to the host's own algebra. If any named block fails B1, the unifying claim is falsified and no downstream result is trusted. This inverts the usual order in the attention-module literature, where a new block is justified by a leaderboard delta first and its mechanism rationalized after.

The second recommendation is to validate disentanglement and identifiability before any accuracy claim. A three-axis operator is only meaningful if the channel, spatial, and identity gates carry separable, non-redundant signal; otherwise CARO is an over-parameterized relabeling of one gate. We gate accuracy reporting behind three controls: (1) the axis-factorial on-off cube (8 cells) with an orthogonality / decorrelation test on the learned gates, confirming each axis helps on its own target regime; (2) a placebo with random gates matched in marginal distribution, isolating mechanism from noise; and (3) a capacity- and FLOP-matched widened-host control, ruling out that any gain is merely added capacity. Only after disentanglement survives these controls does a per-axis accuracy comparison become interpretable; until then a top-1 delta is treated as uninterpretable.

The third recommendation is claim discipline: every accuracy and efficiency statement stays `[conjectured->measured]`, never asserted. We deliberately separate targeted per-axis effects -- channel gating on texture/category, spatial gating on localization, identity gating on skip strength -- from head-dominated aggregate top-1 accuracy, because an aggregate number can move for reasons unrelated to the mechanism (capacity, regularization, the classifier head). Per-axis attribution with a low gate-correlation matrix is the evidence that the operator does what its construction claims; aggregate accuracy is reported alongside, not as the headline. CARO thus offers a guaranteed floor (do-no-harm at init, `[provable-by-construction]`) and a measured upside (any win over SE/CBAM/ECA/Non-local at matched FLOPs, `[conjectured->measured]`) -- an asymmetry that makes adoption safe even when the upside is modest or null.

The fourth and most transferable insight is that the pattern -- unify hand-wired single-axis modulation into one input-conditioned operator with exact collapse identities -- generalizes well beyond CNNs. Any architecture that accretes bolted-on, statically-placed gating or attention modules exhibits a HASM-shaped gap: transformers stack a growing zoo of attention variants (local, global, sparse, linear) at hand-chosen layers; graph neural networks bolt distinct message-passing and gating modules onto each layer. The reusable recipe is (a) name the single missing operator whose limiting gate settings recover the existing modules, (b) initialize it to an identity default (budget = 0) so it is provably do-no-harm at insertion `[provable-by-construction]`, (c) add a learned, input-conditioned placement head in place of hand-wiring or NAS, and (d) ship a B1-style collapse unit test so each prior module is recovered as a falsifiable special case. This turns "yet another module" into a discipline of do-no-harm generalization, improving reproducibility, easing ablation, and lowering the risk of adopting new blocks in production.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Here are the five core questions:

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the channel, spatial, and identity/skip recalibration modules that current CNNs hand-wire as separate, statically-placed, single-axis blocks (the HASM gap) be replaced by one input-conditioned operator, CARO, that jointly modulates all three axes and learns where to apply modulation, while provably collapsing within floating-point tolerance to a plain conv, a residual block, an SE-ResNet block, a CBAM block, and a non-local attention block at limiting gate settings?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

HASM is not an incidental weakness of one model; it is the default design vocabulary of an entire decade of CNN research. Every architecture cited above -- ResNet, SENet, CBAM, ECA-Net, SKNet, Non-local, ShuffleNet, DenseNet, Inception [5,8,10,13,12,11,9,7,6] -- is a different hand-wired, single-axis, static modulator bolted onto the same convolution base. Naming HASM and replacing it with one input-conditioned operator therefore shifts the PRIMITIVE, not a single model. A result here is the kind of result that re-tools how the next generation of backbones is assembled, rather than adding one more entry to a benchmark leaderboard: if channel gating, spatial gating, and identity gating are three limiting settings of CARO, then the taxonomy of "attention modules" collapses into one operator with knobs.

Second, CARO's input-conditioned LEARNED PLACEMENT directly removes a hidden cost that the community currently pays by hand or by search. Today, deciding WHERE to insert an SE or CBAM block in a backbone is a manual design choice or, when automated, is delegated to expensive NAS [21]. CARO instead carries a learned per-block modulation budget (init 0 = pass-through) and a placement head that predicts, per block and per input, whether spending modulation here is worthwhile. This turns an architecture-search problem into a differentiable, in-training decision, which is both cheaper and interpretable (the budget sparsity tells you which blocks actually needed modulation).

Third, the collapse identity gives a reusable do-no-harm guarantee that is rare in this literature. Because CARO's modulation budget initializes to 0 and the gates default to identity, CARO `[provable-by-construction]` recomputes the host block (plain conv, ResNet residual, SE-ResNet, CBAM, non-local) within floating-point tolerance at limiting gate settings. Practically, this means CARO can be dropped into ANY existing backbone and is provably never worse-by-construction at initialization -- the network starts exactly as the backbone it replaced and can only depart from it if training finds a benefit. Any accuracy or efficiency improvement beyond that floor is positioned strictly as `[conjectured->measured]`, never asserted. That asymmetry (guaranteed floor, measured upside) is what makes CARO safe to adopt at scale.

Fourth, cross-axis composition is currently structurally unreachable, and it may matter most exactly where the source survey's motivating tasks live -- under tight compute and under domain shift. In medical imaging and scene/semantic segmentation, the signal is often spatially sparse and channel-specific at the same time (a small lesion in a particular feature channel at a particular location), so the ability to couple channel and spatial gating from one conditioned operator, and to spend a limited modulation budget only where it pays off, is plausibly more valuable than any single-axis module applied everywhere. By making coupling and placement learnable rather than hand-wired, CARO opens a region of the design space that HASM forecloses.

Finally, the broader implication is methodological. Reframing a pile of competing modules as limiting cases of one collapsible operator is a template that extends beyond CNNs: it offers a principled way to consolidate the ever-growing zoo of attention and gating variants into operators with provable boundary behavior, which improves reproducibility, eases ablation (each prior module is recovered exactly as a unit test), and lowers the risk of adopting a new block in production. The immediate payoff is for CNN backbones; the transferable payoff is a discipline of do-no-harm generalization.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The naive reading of CARO is "just put a channel gate, a spatial gate, and a skip gate on every block and let SGD sort it out." That naive approach fails in at least seven concrete, separately-testable ways. Each failure mode below is mapped to a specific design safeguard and to the ablation that would expose it.

1. Naive joint gating EXPLODES parameter and compute cost and breaks the FNN -> CNN efficiency motivation. Three full-rank gate heads per block, conditioned on the full tensor, can rival the host block's own cost and undo the entire reason CNNs replaced fully connected nets. SAFEGUARD: keep every gate head lightweight -- channel gate = GAP followed by a small bottleneck MLP (or a 1D conv over channels, the ECA-Net limit); spatial gate = a single depthwise conv over the channel-pooled map; identity/placement = a scalar linear head on GAP(X). ABLATION: FLOPs/param accounting per head and a FLOP-matched comparison (point 7).

2. Without an identity-default initialization, inserting CARO PERTURBS a converged or pretrained backbone at init and destroys do-no-harm. If gates start at random values, Y != X + F(X) at step 0, so wrapping a pretrained ResNet immediately degrades it. SAFEGUARD: beta init 0, so every gate's deviation from identity is multiplied by 0 and Y reduces to the host exactly (Section 2, B1). The associated claim is a measured non-inferiority-at-init: accuracy of the wrapped backbone equals the host within noise before any CARO training. ABLATION: budget-init 0 vs random-init do-no-harm test.

3. Coupled gates can collapse to trivial all-pass (gates -> 1, modulation never used) or all-suppress (gates -> 0, signal killed) -- "dead modulation" where the operator learns to ignore itself or to over-attenuate. SAFEGUARD: a budget regularizer on beta (encourage small but nonzero spend) plus the placement head pi that decides per block whether to spend budget at all, so the operator is pushed to use modulation where it pays and abstain elsewhere rather than saturating. ABLATION: learned-placement sparsity metric and a placebo with random gates matched in marginal distribution.

4. Learned PLACEMENT is discrete and non-differentiable. "Which blocks should modulate" is naturally a binary per-block decision, and optimizing it directly is a combinatorial NAS problem -- expensive and yielding no collapse guarantee. SAFEGUARD: a continuous relaxation -- a soft, per-block budget pi * beta in [0, inf) that SGD can anneal toward 0 or positive, rather than a hard discrete search. ABLATION: learned (soft) vs hand-wired placement; report the fraction of blocks that end with active budget.

5. Cross-axis coupling risks TRAINING INSTABILITY. Because g_s is conditioned on the channel-pooled (effectively g_c-influenced) tensor and the gates multiply, gradients couple multiplicatively and early training can oscillate or diverge. SAFEGUARD: a staged / warm-start schedule -- hold beta at 0 (host-equivalent) for a warm-up, then release the budget so coupling turns on after the backbone features are stable. ABLATION: cross-axis coupling on/off and warm-start on/off training-stability curves.

6. IDENTIFIABILITY -- the three axes might secretly be three relabelings of one gate. If the channel, spatial, and identity gates carry redundant signal, CARO would be over-parameterized and its "three-axis" story would be vacuous. SAFEGUARD: an axis-factorial design plus a decorrelation/orthogonality test on the learned gates, demonstrating each axis carries SEPARABLE, non-redundant signal that helps on its own target regime. ABLATION: the channel/spatial/identity on-off cube with a per-axis attribution and a gate-correlation matrix.

7. The win can be CONFOUNDED by added capacity. Any accuracy gain could merely reflect the extra parameters/FLOPs of the gate heads rather than the CARO mechanism. SAFEGUARD: a capacity/FLOP-matched plain backbone control -- a host net widened to match CARO's parameter and FLOP budget -- so a CARO win must survive equal-capacity comparison. ABLATION: FLOP-matched and param-matched controls reported alongside every accuracy number.

Bottom line: the difficulty is NOT inventing a channel, spatial, or skip gate -- each already exists in isolation. The difficulty is making all three coexist as ONE input-conditioned operator that (a) stays cheap, (b) is provably do-no-harm at init, (c) does not degenerate to dead modulation, (d) learns placement differentiably, (e) trains stably under multiplicative cross-axis coupling, (f) is identifiable as three separable axes, and (g) wins only when capacity is matched. CARO's budget-gated, identity-default, coupled, placement-learned parameterization is precisely the set of safeguards that turns the naive stack into a collapsible, falsifiable operator. Every safeguard above has a matching ablation, so each claimed failure mode is empirically checkable rather than asserted.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The first barrier is sociological-but-real: the three families grew up in SEPARATE sub-communities with different objective functions, and were benchmarked as COMPETING modules rather than as facets of one operator. The IDENTITY/SKIP line [5,6,7] was driven by the very-deep-network optimization problem; the CHANNEL line [8,13,9] was driven by efficiency and cheap recalibration; the SPATIAL/attention line [10,11,12] was driven by long-range context and "where to look." Because a new module was almost always published by showing it BEATS the others on top-1 accuracy at comparable FLOPs, the framing was inherently adversarial -- SE vs CBAM vs ECA -- not unificatory. Nobody had an incentive to ask "what single operator has all of these as limiting cases," so the unifying question was never the unit of work.

The second barrier is that the naive union does not work and there was no construction to make it work. Simply stacking SE + CBAM + a residual block ADDS parameters and compute and can hurt accuracy, because each added gate perturbs a backbone that was already tuned. The closest prior attempt, CBAM [10], is instructive: it DOES compose channel and spatial attention in sequence, proving the community knew the axes could be combined -- but it stays hand-wired and static, applies its gates everywhere it is inserted, and crucially has NO identity-default / budget=0 construction, so it carries no do-no-harm guarantee and never reduces to a collapsible canonical operator. Without the modulation-budget-initialized-to-0 and gates-default-to-identity construction that CARO uses, any combined-attention block is a net-new module with its own risk at initialization, not a strict generalization. That missing construction is exactly why "just combine them" never produced a collapsible operator.

The third barrier is that learned PLACEMENT was historically off-loaded to Neural Architecture Search [21]. Deciding where in a backbone to spend modulation was treated as a discrete architecture-search problem -- expensive, opaque, and producing a fixed found architecture rather than an input-conditioned, in-training decision. NAS yields a placement but no interpretable collapse-to-canonical guarantee, and its cost put principled placement out of reach for most practitioners. CARO instead relaxes placement into a continuous, learned per-block budget with a placement head, sidestepping discrete search while keeping the collapse identity intact.

The fourth barrier is that the one line which DID add input-conditioning conditioned the wrong thing. CondConv [16] and Dynamic Convolution [17] generate the convolution KERNEL weights per input as an attention-weighted mixture of expert kernels; deformable convolution [18] conditions the sampling geometry. These are genuine input-conditioning advances, but they modulate the kernel/geometry, NOT the coupled channel/spatial/identity recalibration gates, and none of them proves collapse to SE-ResNet, CBAM, or the plain residual block. So even the input-conditioned quadrant of prior work stopped short: it had conditioning without axis-unification, while the attention families had (partial) axis-modulation without conditioning or collapse. The quadrant that is simultaneously axis-unifying, input-conditioned, learned-placement, AND provably collapsible -- the quadrant CARO occupies -- stayed empty. The near-misses (CBAM for composition, CondConv/DynamicConv for conditioning, NAS for placement) each supply one ingredient; none supplies the do-no-harm collapsible operator, which is the exact differentiation.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

### Method

CARO is a drop-in block that replaces a residual / SE / CBAM block in any standard backbone without changing the surrounding stem, downsampling, or classifier. The forward pass for input X in R^(C x H x W) and host function F is:

```
  g_c  = sigmoid( MLP( GAP(X) ) )            # channel, in [0,1]^C
  g_s  = sigmoid( DWConv( pool_channel(X) ) )# spatial, in [0,1]^(H x W)
  g_id = sigmoid( w_id . GAP(X) + b_id )     # identity/skip, in [0,1]
  pi   = sigmoid( w_pi . GAP(X) + b_pi )     # placement, in [0,1]
  s    = pi * beta                           # per-block budget spend, beta init 0

  g~_c  = 1 + s * (g_c - 1)
  g~_s  = 1 + s * (g_s - 1)
  g~_id =     s *  g_id
  Y     = X + g~_id (x) ( g~_c (x) g~_s (x) F(X) )
```

Budget + placement: beta is a single learned scalar per block initialized to 0 (modulation OFF at init); pi is an input-conditioned scalar deciding whether to spend budget here. A budget regularizer lambda * sum_blocks |beta_block| keeps spend sparse. Training schedule: warm-start with beta frozen at 0 (host-equivalent) for the first E_warm epochs, then unfreeze beta and pi so cross-axis coupling and placement turn on after backbone features stabilize. Transform-free / lightweight constraints: gate heads use only GAP, a channel bottleneck MLP (or 1D conv), and a single depthwise conv -- no full-tensor dense projections, no extra spatial resolution, no auxiliary backbone -- so per-block overhead stays small relative to F.

### Datasets and models

- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 (classification, small-image regime).
- ImageNet-1k (classification, large-scale regime).
- Backbones: ResNet-50 and ResNet-18 (standard residual hosts), plus an efficient backbone -- MobileNetV2 (and optionally EfficientNet-B0) -- to test CARO under tight compute.
- Optional transfer to echo the source paper's motivating tasks: semantic segmentation on ADE20K (or Cityscapes), and a medical-imaging classification set such as ISIC 2019 skin-lesion or the CheXpert chest-X-ray benchmark.

### Baselines

- Plain ResNet (host, no modulation).
- SE-ResNet (CHANNEL axis only).
- CBAM-ResNet (channel-then-spatial).
- ECA-ResNet (1D-conv channel gate).
- Non-local net (SPATIAL/affinity axis only).
- NAIVE stacked-all-three module: SE + CBAM + residual gates stacked and learned jointly with NO budget, NO identity-default, hand-wired placement -- the mechanism null. CARO must be no worse and ideally better than this null to show the value is the parameterization, not merely "having three gates."
- Capacity/FLOP-matched plain backbone: host widened to CARO's param and FLOP budget -- rules out "the win is just added capacity."
- B1 collapse-recovery unit test: a correctness GATE (not an accuracy baseline) -- every named architecture must be recovered within float eps before any accuracy number is trusted.

All accuracy comparisons are run at equal-FLOP / equal-param / equal-budget settings.

### Metrics

- Top-1 and top-5 accuracy (CIFAR / ImageNet); mIoU for segmentation transfer.
- Params, FLOPs, and wall-clock latency (CPU and GPU).
- Collapse-fidelity error: max | Y_CARO - Y_host | per named architecture, which must be <= float eps at boundary settings (the B1 metric -- the core NEW metric).
- Learned-placement sparsity: fraction of blocks ending with active budget (beta_block > tau).
- Per-axis attribution: contribution of channel / spatial / identity gates, plus a gate-correlation matrix for the identifiability test.
- Robustness/transfer metrics where relevant (e.g. corruption robustness, transfer accuracy/mIoU on the medical or segmentation set).

If a medical-imaging transfer dataset is included, aggregate accuracy is reported alongside subgroup and out-of-distribution performance; no result is interpreted as evidence of diagnostic readiness. ImageNet-1k and CIFAR results reflect benchmark-distribution performance; generalization to under-represented geographic domains or rare visual categories is not implied and is left as an open empirical question. The identity-default initialization (beta=0) and learned sparse placement head make CARO output-equivalent to the host backbone at initialization (the lightweight gate heads add only small overhead), and the expected sparse active-block fraction [conjectured->measured] suggests lower amortized overhead than naive stacking of SE and CBAM modules.

### Ablations

- Boundary-case recovery (B1): each named architecture -- plain conv, ResNet residual, SE-ResNet, CBAM, Non-local, ECA-Net -- recovered by construction; report collapse-fidelity error per case. [provable-by-construction]
- Axis factorial: the channel / spatial / identity on-off cube (8 cells) with an orthogonality / decorrelation test to confirm the three axes carry separable, non-redundant signal.
- Budget-init 0 vs random init: do-no-harm test measuring accuracy of the wrapped backbone at init.
- Learned vs hand-wired placement: soft per-block budget vs fixed insertion.
- Cross-axis coupling on/off: g_s conditioned on channel-pool vs independent gates.
- Capacity-matched control: CARO vs FLOP/param-matched widened host.
- Placebo: random gates matched in marginal distribution -- isolates mechanism from noise.

### Expected outcomes

All accuracy/efficiency outcomes are `[conjectured->measured]` -- promised to be measured, never asserted:

- CARO matches or modestly beats the best single-axis module (SE / CBAM / ECA / Non-local) at comparable FLOPs. [conjectured->measured]
- CARO is NEVER worse than the host backbone at init -- this is provable (beta = 0 -> Y = host, B1), and the at-init non-inferiority is measured to confirm the construction holds end-to-end. [provable-by-construction] for the identity; [conjectured->measured] for the post-training comparison.
- Learned placement concentrates modulation in a SPARSE subset of blocks (low placement-sparsity fraction), removing hand-tuning of where to insert SE/CBAM blocks. [conjectured->measured]
- Per-axis attribution shows each axis helps on its target regime (channel on texture/category, spatial on localization, identity on depth/skip strength), with low cross-gate correlation. [conjectured->measured]
- CARO beats the NAIVE stacked-all-three null and survives the capacity-matched control, showing the gain is the parameterization, not added capacity. [conjectured->measured]
